does: This paper demonstrates a systematic vulnerability in GPT-4's safety mechanisms by translating harmful English prompts into low-resource languages and measuring attack success rates. The authors evaluate their approach on AdvBenchmark, finding that translated prompts achieve a 79% success rate in eliciting unsafe content from GPT-4—comparable to state-of-the-art jailbreaking techniques. The core finding is that safety training's linguistic inequality creates exploitable gaps: low-resource languages show dramatically higher vulnerability compared to high- and mid-resource languages, indicating the safety mechanisms are substantially weaker in these languages. The paper documents how publicly available translation APIs can facilitate these attacks, making the vulnerability practically exploitable by any user regardless of technical sophistication or language proficiency.
connects: This work builds on growing literature documenting adversarial vulnerabilities in large language models, extending beyond single-language jailbreaking attacks to expose cross-lingual safety failures. It responds to prior research on the unequal distribution of NLP capabilities across languages, showing how linguistic disparities—previously understood primarily as fairness issues affecting speakers of low-resource languages—create new security risks. The paper connects to red-teaming and safety evaluation research that has identified multiple attack vectors against LLM safeguards, while also engaging with alignment research examining how safety training generalizes across different model capabilities and deployment contexts. The work implicitly addresses limitations in how safety procedures like RLHF have been trained predominantly on high-resource languages.
enables: This paper opens several research directions for AI safety. It motivates the need for multilingual safety training and red-teaming protocols that adequately cover low-resource languages to prevent simple translation-based circumvention. The work enables future research into understanding which safety mechanisms fail under language variation and why, potentially informing more robust approaches that maintain consistency across linguistic boundaries. It points toward developing better mechanisms for evaluating and ensuring cross-lingual safety alignment in deployed systems. The findings also suggest investigating whether other model vulnerabilities correlate with linguistic representation in training data, and whether similar translation-based attacks work for other safety-critical systems beyond content generation.
